title: Implement batch polynomial operations for extension fields
description:
Implement batch and vectorized polynomial operations over arbitrary `FiniteField` types, extending the algebraic toolkit beyond single-element arithmetic to efficient polynomial-level computations.

## Scope

### Core polynomial type

```rust
/// A univariate polynomial over a finite field.
/// Coefficients are stored in ascending degree order: [c₀, c₁, ..., cₙ]
/// represents c₀ + c₁·x + c₂·x² + ... + cₙ·xⁿ.
pub struct FieldPoly<F: FiniteField> {
    coeffs: Vec<F>,
}
```

### Operations

| Operation | Description | Complexity |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| `evaluate(&self, point: &F)` | Horner's method evaluation | O(n) multiplications |
| `batch_evaluate(&self, points: &[F])` | Evaluate at multiple points | O(n·k) or O(n log² n) with subproduct tree |
| `mul(&self, other: &Self)` | Polynomial multiplication | O(n²) schoolbook, O(n log n) FFT |
| `batch_mul(polys: &[Self])` | Product of multiple polynomials | O(n log n · log k) via binary tree |
| `gcd(&self, other: &Self)` | Polynomial GCD (Euclidean algorithm) | O(n²) |
| `batch_gcd(polys: &[Self])` | GCD of multiple polynomials | Pairwise reduction |
| `interpolate(points: &[(F, F)])` | Lagrange interpolation | O(n²) or O(n log² n) |

### Batch evaluation via subproduct tree

For evaluating a degree-n polynomial at k points, the subproduct tree method achieves O(n log² n) using:

1. Build a binary tree of subproducts: `M_i = (x - x_i)` at leaves, products at internal nodes.
2. Top-down remainder computation: reduce the polynomial modulo each subproduct.
3. Leaf values are the evaluations.

This is critical for BCH/Reed-Solomon decoding where a single error-locator polynomial must be evaluated at all field elements.

### NTT-based multiplication (for suitable fields)

When the field supports efficient NTT (Number Theoretic Transform) — specifically when the multiplicative group order has a large power-of-2 factor — polynomial multiplication can use:

1. NTT of both polynomials (O(n log n))
2. Pointwise multiplication (O(n))
3. Inverse NTT (O(n log n))

This requires Proth primes (k·2^n + 1) or fields with primitive roots of unity of sufficient order.

## Integration with existing code

The current `gf2m/` module has polynomial operations hardcoded for `Gf2mElement`. This new module provides generic versions:
- `Gf2mPoly` operations can delegate to `FieldPoly<Gf2mElement>` (or remain specialized for performance)
- BCH decoding in `gf2-coding` can eventually use generic polynomial operations

## Location

`crates/gf2-core/src/field/poly.rs` (new file in the field module)

## Dependencies

- bfe0ba7b (FiniteField trait) — all polynomial operations are generic over `F: FiniteField`

## Success criteria

- Polynomial multiplication is correct: `(a·b)(x) == a(x)·b(x)` for all x (property test).
- Horner evaluation matches naive sum-of-terms evaluation.
- Batch evaluation via subproduct tree produces identical results to individual Horner evaluations.
- Interpolation round-trips: `interpolate(points).evaluate(x_i) == y_i` for all input points.
- Benchmark: subproduct-tree batch evaluation is faster than k individual Horner evaluations for k ≥ 16, n ≥ 16.
